questions.q: What is the primary mechanism by which TCP Reno detects congestion?
options: Timeout expiration or receipt of three duplicate acknowledgments indicating packet loss
An explicit congestion notification (ECN) bit set in the IP header by routers
A decrease in the round-trip time (RTT) measured by the sender
Receiving ICMP Source Quench messages from intermediate routers
questions.explanation: TCP Reno relies on packet loss as a congestion signal, detected either by timeout (no ACK received) or by three duplicate ACKs (fast retransmit). While ECN exists as a more explicit signal, traditional Reno predates it and uses loss detection. RTT changes are not reliable congestion indicators, and Source Quench is obsolete.

questions.q: In TCP slow start, how does the congestion window (cwnd) grow?
options: Exponentially: cwnd increases by one MSS per ACK received (doubling each RTT) until reaching ssthresh
Linearly: cwnd increases by one MSS per RTT
In steps of one half of the previous cwnd per RTT
Adaptively based on actual network bandwidth measurement
questions.explanation: Slow start grows cwnd exponentially (each ACK increments cwnd by 1 MSS, resulting in doubling per RTT) until ssthresh is reached. This aggressive growth ensures quick utilization of available bandwidth while monitoring for loss. Linear growth occurs in congestion avoidance, not slow start. Bandwidth measurement is not part of standard algorithms.

questions.q: What is the Additive Increase, Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD) principle in congestion control?
options: Increase cwnd by one MSS per RTT during normal operation; halve it upon detecting congestion (timeout reduces it to 1 MSS)
Increase cwnd by multiplying previous value; decrease by adding a fixed amount when congestion occurs
Wait for explicit congestion signals from the network before making any adjustments
Maintain a fixed cwnd and rely only on ACK timing to detect problems
questions.explanation: AIMD is the core strategy of TCP's congestion avoidance phase: additive increase (linear growth, safer than exponential) in good conditions, multiplicative decrease (halving cwnd or resetting to 1 MSS) upon loss. This balances responsiveness and stability. The inverse behavior would be unstable, and fixed windows offer no adaptation.

questions.q: How does TCP Cubic differ from Reno in its congestion control behavior?
options: Cubic uses a cubic function for cwnd growth to recover faster from congestion while being more aggressive at high bandwidth links than Reno
Cubic operates at the network layer while Reno operates at the transport layer
Cubic eliminates the need for the three-way handshake
Cubic reduces latency by disabling acknowledgments during recovery
questions.explanation: TCP Cubic (used by Linux and modern systems) replaces Reno's linear congestion avoidance with a cubic polynomial function. This allows faster recovery and better utilization on high-bandwidth, high-latency links while being less bursty than Reno. Both are transport-layer protocols, and neither changes the handshake or ACK mechanism.

questions.q: What is fast retransmit and how does it improve upon timeout-based retransmission?
options: Upon receiving 3 duplicate ACKs, immediately retransmit the lost segment without waiting for a timeout, reducing latency and recovery time
A method of using error-correcting codes to avoid retransmission entirely
Sending multiple copies of each segment preemptively to guarantee delivery
Reducing the timeout value to detect losses faster
questions.explanation: Fast retransmit triggers on three duplicate ACKs (indicating the receiver has seen packets beyond the lost one) rather than waiting for RTO (typically 100ms+). This allows sub-second recovery in many cases. Error correction, preemptive sending, and lower timeouts are not the fast retransmit mechanism; very low timeouts can cause spurious retransmissions.

questions.q: What is ssthresh (slow start threshold) and how is it modified?
options: The congestion window size at which slow start transitions to congestion avoidance; upon packet loss, it is set to half the cwnd at the time of loss
A fixed limit on the maximum transmission rate regardless of network conditions
The minimum amount of data that must be buffered before transmission begins
A timer value controlling the rate at which ACKs are sent
questions.explanation: ssthresh marks the boundary between exponential and linear growth. When loss occurs, ssthresh becomes half the previous cwnd (or cwnd at loss), and cwnd resets (timeout) or reduces (fast retransmit). ssthresh is not a hard limit on transmission; rather, it dynamically adapts based on congestion history. It is not a buffer size or ACK timer.

questions.q: What is bufferbloat and how does it affect congestion control?
options: Excessive buffering in routers causes high queuing latency, masking congestion loss signals and preventing TCP from adjusting cwnd appropriately
A failure of TCP to buffer incoming segments, causing unnecessary retransmissions
An overflow of sender buffers when cwnd is set too large
A protocol error where packets are duplicated in network buffers
questions.explanation: Bufferbloat occurs when routers (or network equipment) have large buffers that absorb packets rather than dropping them, hiding congestion. TCP's loss-based signals are delayed or absent, so cwnd grows unchecked, increasing latency without reducing the sending rate. Modern solutions include Active Queue Management (AQM) and ECN to provide explicit signals before buffers overflow.

questions.q: In the context of TCP congestion control, what does retransmission timeout (RTO) represent?
options: The duration the sender waits for an ACK before assuming the segment was lost and retransmitting; calculated from RTT and RTT variance
The round-trip time minus one standard deviation
The fixed interval at which all segments must be retransmitted
The time required for a packet to traverse from sender to the network backbone
questions.explanation: RTO is dynamically calculated using SRTT (smoothed RTT) and RTTVAR: RTO = SRTT + 4 * RTTVAR, with typical initial value of 1 second and minimum of 200ms. Timeout triggers cwnd reset to 1 MSS (the most aggressive congestion response). RTO is not simply RTT minus variance, nor is it fixed across all segments.

questions.q: What is the recovery phase in TCP Reno and how does it work?
options: After fast retransmit, cwnd is set to ssthresh+3 MSS and increases by 1 MSS per duplicate ACK (inflating), then returns to normal congestion avoidance after one RTT
The phase where cwnd grows exponentially in slow start
A period where the sender stops transmitting entirely until all buffers clear
The phase where TCP waits for the RTO timer to expire before resuming transmission
questions.explanation: TCP Reno's recovery (fast recovery) phase inflates cwnd during fast retransmit to keep the pipe full while waiting for the retransmitted segment's ACK. This reduces unnecessary blocking. Exponential growth is slow start; complete cessation or RTO-based recovery would cause unnecessary delays. Fast recovery is specific to the fast retransmit path.

questions.q: How does ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) improve upon loss-based congestion control?
options: ECN allows routers to mark packets under congestion without dropping them, giving TCP explicit, early signals to reduce cwnd before loss occurs, reducing retransmissions and latency
ECN guarantees zero packet loss by buffering all marked packets at the router
ECN replaces the need for ACKs by using packet marks as delivery confirmation
ECN allows senders to increase cwnd more aggressively since losses no longer indicate congestion
questions.explanation: ECN (RFC 3168) enables routers to set bits in packet headers (CE) instead of dropping packets. The receiver echoes this signal back (ECE), prompting the sender to reduce cwnd as if loss occurred, but without actual loss and retransmission. This cuts unnecessary retransmissions and achieves lower latency. ECN does not eliminate ACKs or allow aggressive growth; it complements loss-based signals.
